posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
This is terrible advice to anyone thinking of going to wine events to learn about regions. Take Burgundy, which is what’s being discussed here: the only way to know which are the “top” producers is to taste, and taste a lot. Burgundy, especially, can change over the course of a few years (the production is small enough!). Producers change styles, vintages play into some producers’ styles but against others, generational shifts result in changes (2009 Favieley is nothing like the 2005 Faiveley), producers change oak regimens (2010 Paul Pillot and 2017 Paul Pillot taste quite different!), producers change certain cuvees because plots come online as the vines get older, certain cuvees change because a metayage is lost, etc. etc.

People who dismiss less famous producers as “the bottom 2/3” (whatever that means) are often more interested in drinking labels than what’s the wines. I would add that, of course, the bottom 2/3 aren’t coming to the grand tasting at Paulee anyway. Daniel, Raj and their team aren’t inviting producers who make genuinely bad wine, whatever my views on Duband and Lamarche are

Andrew_K:

You really don’t need to sample the bottom 2/3 of producers.

posts.name: Greg Kahn
posts.cooked:
Vivek_K:

Thoughts on any major wine events (especially this fall) that are “worth it” for a berserker? For example was looking at the New York wine experience, decanter events, etc.

Honest answer - depends on what regions you like and what your level of interest is. If you’re getting into a specific region like Bordeaux, the New York wine experience is more worth it, because their Bordeaux presence is strong - but their Burgundy presence is minimal. But for a general fun walkaround with some famous wines, it’s great. If you’re into regions in particularly, some of the focused events like Paulee for Burgundy (I would really strongly recommend the verticals), UGC for Bordeaux (if you’re prepared to taste 60 young Bordeaux), Fete du Champagne are fantastic.
And if you really want a fascinating look into issues in wine today, I think the symposium @William_Kelley put together is going to be fabulous (and the wines are going to be great).

posts.name: Loren Supp
posts.cooked:
beyond bdx, I’ve found the NYWE a great way to learn more about Spanish and Italian wines. Especially the latter. Very well represented.

To be honest, I’ve had the most fun moving horizontally between regions with the same varietals at the event. I.e. “what are the differences between high end napa vs washington vs bdx cab/merlot”Often the notion of terroir is challenged - at least with regards to singularity. Also found wines that I fell in love with that I’d never tried through this methodology (Klipsun being one, Haut Brion another)

posts.name: Rick Allen
posts.cooked:
International Pinot Noir Celebration. 70+ Pinot Noir producers pouring, mainly from Oregon, but with a smattering from elsewhere (Bize, Dujac, Meo-Camuzet, Jadot and others from Burgundy, Henri Giruad and Laurent Perrier from Champagne). The main events are the Grand Dinner on Friday night, and the Salmon Bake on Saturday night.

At the Salmon Bake, the wines that the somms pull out are pretty good (for our table, 2009 Lioco Chardonnay Demuth, 2012 Evening Lands Chardonnay La Source), but the wine that the participants bring and share are over the top. I was mainly pouring beer, but I still tasted Vilmart (margnum), Krug, 2005 Thomas, 2017 Walter Scott Cuvee Anne (magnum), and Sandar and Hem Bald Mountain Chardonnay. We also had Brian and Jill from Belle Pente sitting with us, and they brought quite a bit of wine. I understand that the somms pour better wines at the Grand Dinner, and there’s still a bunch of sharing that goes on.

The whole effect is that of a giant two day party celebrating Pinot Noir.
